food_subgroup: Sharing platter — mixed fried and grilled items with deep-fried sides

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for this specific multi-component Taiwanese-fusion sharing platter. FOODON_03315116 (mixed dish food product) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FMM (Mixed dishes) is the closest parent category. No specific code exists for this multi-component fusion platter.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept exists for this specific multi-component sharing platter.

main_ingredients: four-cheese blend (cheddar, mozzarella, parmesan, cream cheese)

serving.natural_language_portion: One sharing platter (approximately 600 g) intended for 2–3 people, comprising fried fish bites, lime and sriracha chicken skewers, spicy lamb meatballs, four-cheese wontons, and a portion of fries.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component foods: fried fish fillet, grilled chicken skewer, lamb meatball (fried), fried wonton with cheese filling, french fries (deep-fried)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this proprietary multi-component platter. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of five component categories using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference entries: (1) fried fish fillet (~20% by weight, USDA FDC #174204 Fish, battered or breaded, fried), (2) grilled chicken skewer (~20%, USDA FDC #171477 Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, roasted), (3) lamb meatball fried (~20%, USDA FDC #174357 Lamb, ground, cooked, pan-browned), (4) fried wonton with cheese (~15%, USDA FDC #168927 Wontons, fried, with meat filling adapted with cheese), (5) french fries deep-fried (~25%, USDA FDC #170720 Potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, as purchased). Weights are approximate equal-portion assumptions. Confidence is low due to proprietary recipe and restaurant-specific preparation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate based on mixed fried foods and potato fries composite.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this proprietary platter. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on weighted composite of component GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008: deep-fried fish (GI ~38, analogous to battered fish), grilled chicken (GI ~0, protein-dominant, negligible glycemic contribution), lamb meatballs (GI ~0, protein-dominant), cheese wontons (GI ~45, analogous to fried wonton wrappers with filling), french fries (GI ~63 for deep-fried potato). Weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution per component. Composite GI ≈ 52. GL per 600 g serving = (52 × 134.4 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 70. GI category: low (≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg/100 g; ~2880 mg per 600 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this platter due to its very high sodium load (~2880 mg per serving, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg), primarily from sriracha sauce, cheese, seasoned breadcrumbs, and deep-frying in salted oil.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~52) but high glycemic load per serving (~70 GL units) due to large carbohydrate portion from fries and wonton wrappers
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this platter with caution: while the GI is low-moderate, the large serving size delivers a high glycemic load (~70 GL), and the high fat content from deep-frying may impair postprandial insulin sensitivity; portion control and pairing with non-starchy vegetables is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (14.8 g/100 g), saturated fat (4.9 g/100 g), trans fat (0.2 g/100 g), and sodium (~480 mg/100 g) from deep-frying and cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this platter due to its combination of high saturated fat, trans fat from deep-frying, and very high sodium, all of which are associated with elevated LDL cholesterol, arterial stiffness, and increased cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Full Spread is a multi-component sharing platter served at Riverside Buns (Boat Quay, Singapore), a Taiwanese-inspired fusion restaurant. It comprises five distinct items: (1) Fried Fish Bites — bite-sized white fish pieces coated in seasoned breadcrumbs and deep-fried to a golden crisp; (2) Lime & Sriracha Chicken Skewers — grilled chicken breast marinated in lime juice and sriracha chilli sauce, served on skewers with a smoky, tangy heat; (3) Spicy Lamb Meatballs — ground lamb seasoned with cumin, chilli, and coriander, pan-fried or baked to a juicy, spiced finish; (4) Four Cheese Wontons — crispy deep-fried wonton parcels filled with a blend of four cheeses (typically cheddar, mozzarella, parmesan, and cream cheese), offering a rich, gooey interior; and (5) Fries — classic deep-fried potato strips. The platter is designed for sharing among 2–3 diners and is priced at SGD 52. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense (~268 kcal/100 g), high in protein (13.5 g/100 g) from the fish, chicken, and lamb components, and high in fat (14.8 g/100 g) due to deep-frying. Sodium content is high (~480 mg/100 g), making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), but the glycemic load per full serving is high (~70 GL units) due to the fries and wonton wrappers. Clinically, this dish is best consumed occasionally and in moderated portions by individuals managing cardiometabolic conditions.

confidence_reason: This is a proprietary restaurant-specific sharing platter with no direct entry in any approved primary nutrient database (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of five USDA SR Legacy component food analogues. GI is estimated from analogous component foods using the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is low due to the proprietary recipe, unknown exact ingredient ratios, and restaurant-specific preparation methods.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component analogues; no direct approved-source entry exists for this proprietary platter. 2. Serving size of 600 g is an estimate for a 2–3 person sharing platter at SGD 52; actual weight may vary. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 4. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is estimated from deep-frying in partially hydrogenated or standard vegetable oil; actual value depends on restaurant oil type. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; lamb and chicken sourcing is unknown. 6. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. GL per 600 g serving calculated as (52 × 134.4 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 70. 7. The cuisine tag 'Taiwanese' from the source is retained but the dish is best described as Taiwanese-inspired fusion served in Singapore. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component analogues and carry low confidence. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation.
